title: Cocycles, Lyapunov exponents, localization
speaker: Wilhelm Schlag
affiliation: University of Chicago; Visiting Professor, School of Mathematics
abstract: This talk will be an introduction to the methods used in the study of spectral properties of Schroedinger operators with a potential defined via the action of an ergodic transformation. Open problems relating to Lyapunov exponents over a skew shift base will be discussed.

proof_stages.label: Ergodic matrix cocycles and Lyapunov exponents
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up measurable matrix cocycles over an ergodic transformation and derives the Lyapunov exponent as the subadditive limit of averaged logarithmic norms of monodromy products.

proof_stages.label: Positive exponents, Oseledets directions, and Furstenberg positivity
proof_stages.summary: A positive two-dimensional Lyapunov exponent gives Oseledets contracting lines, while Furstenberg’s iid criterion explains positivity qualitatively but not in the quantitative form needed for correlated models or localization.

proof_stages.label: Schrödinger transfer matrices and Herman’s subharmonic bound
proof_stages.summary: The quasi-periodic discrete Schrödinger equation is encoded as an SL(2,R) cocycle, and Herman’s complexification trick uses subharmonicity to prove the lower bound L(E) >= log lambda for the cosine cocycle.

proof_stages.label: Almost-Mathieu spectral phase picture and classical localization
proof_stages.summary: The Harper/almost-Mathieu operator is related to periodic Floquet theory, Kotani theory, the lambda phase transition, and Jitomirskaya’s Anderson localization theorem for Diophantine frequency and lambda>1.

proof_stages.label: Failure of the soft Oseledets proof and resonance control
proof_stages.summary: The tempting proof that stable and unstable Oseledets lines force localization fails because exceptional sets depend on energy, so the actual proof must track resonances and prevent infinite tunneling through multiscale perturbation.

proof_stages.label: Bourgain-Goldstein nonperturbative localization mechanism
proof_stages.summary: Bourgain and Goldstein’s theorem obtains Anderson localization from a uniform positive Lyapunov exponent using large deviations, subharmonicity, and semi-algebraic complexity bounds to exclude multiple resonances for almost every frequency.

proof_stages.label: Skew-shift Schrödinger cocycle and positivity conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The skew-shift model is introduced, the conjecture L(omega,E)>0 for all energies and all nonzero coupling is formulated, and heuristic/randomness evidence plus known partial localization results are reviewed.

proof_stages.label: Effective and finite-field program via monodromy traces
proof_stages.summary: Ongoing work seeks effective constants in the multiscale scheme and studies finite-field periodic analogues, where Dyson-series exponential sums are expected to show exponential growth of averaged monodromy traces in the skew-shift case.

examples.name: Orthogonal-matrix cocycles with no growth
examples.note: A probability measure supported on orthogonal matrices is used to show concretely why compactness prevents positive Lyapunov growth in Furstenberg-type criteria.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only uses orthogonal matrices as a quick case with norm one and no growth to motivate Furstenberg conditions.

passes.reason: Orthogonal values preserve the Euclidean norm, giving minimal zero Lyapunov growth and showing positivity needs a noncompactness hypothesis.
Orthogonal-valued cocycles are compact isometric cases with zero/minimal Lyapunov growth, used to show positivity needs extra hypotheses.
Orthogonal cocycles are isometric norm-one/minimal-growth cases used to show Lyapunov positivity needs noncompact expansion.
O(d)-valued cocycles are isometric/maximal-compact and attain the minimal zero-growth Lyapunov behavior, used to show positivity needs extra hypotheses.
orthogonal cocycles are norm-preserving isometric cases with minimal zero Lyapunov growth, used to show positivity needs extra hypotheses
orthogonal cocycles are isometric/compact with the minimal zero Lyapunov growth, used to show positivity needs noncompact expansion
orthogonal cocycles are isometric compact-group cases with minimal zero Lyapunov growth, used to show positivity needs noncompact expanding hypotheses
Orthogonal cocycles are invoked to show positivity can fail without a noncompact/expanding condition, and they are isometric/minimal-growth, highly symmetric cocycles.
Orthogonal cocycles are norm-preserving, maximally compact/symmetric minimal-growth cases used to show positivity needs a non-compactness condition.
Used to show positivity can fail without a noncompact/expanding condition; orthogonal cocycles are maximally isometric and have minimal zero growth.
Orthogonal cocycles are isometric, maximally norm-preserving cases with minimal Lyapunov growth, used to show positivity needs noncompact/expanding hypotheses.
Orthogonal-valued cocycles are norm-preserving isometric cases giving the minimal zero growth and showing positivity needs non-compact expansion.

examples.name: Rational-frequency periodic almost-Mathieu operator
examples.note: The rational case is used as a removable Floquet-theoretic benchmark where the monodromy trace produces spectral bands and gaps.
examples.engagement_reason: For rational omega he analyzes the periodic operator via the q-step monodromy, trace discriminant, Floquet bands, eigenvalues, and quasi-momentum.

passes.reason: Rational frequency collapses the rotation to a finite-period Floquet operator with translation symmetry, used as a classical comparison case.
Rational frequency collapses the quasi-periodic almost-Mathieu dynamics to a periodic Floquet problem with translation regularity.
Rational frequency collapses the quasi-periodic rotation to an exactly periodic Floquet problem, used as a contrast rather than a refutation.
Rational frequency collapses the almost-Mathieu dynamics to a finite-period Floquet problem, used as background comparison rather than a refutation.
rational frequency collapses the almost-Mathieu dynamics to a finite-period Floquet problem, used as a contrast rather than a refutation
rational frequency collapses the quasi-periodic almost-Mathieu dynamics to a finite-period Floquet problem
rational frequency collapses the quasiperiodic almost-Mathieu dynamics to a finite-period Floquet-symmetric case
Rational frequency collapses the almost-Mathieu dynamics to a finite-period Floquet problem, used as a contrasting classical case rather than to refute a claim.
Rational frequency collapses the quasi-periodic almost-Mathieu model to a finite-period Floquet case, used as comparison rather than refutation.
Used as the periodic Floquet comparison case; rational frequency collapses the quasiperiodic rotation to an exact finite-period problem.
Rational frequency makes the almost-Mathieu potential periodic with Floquet translational regularity, used as a comparison case rather than to refute a claim.
Rational frequency collapses the quasiperiodic almost-Mathieu dynamics to a periodic Floquet problem with exact lattice-period symmetry, used mainly as a contrast case.

examples.name: Continuum quasi-periodic Schrödinger/PDE contrast
examples.note: Prompted by audience questions, the continuum case illustrates why the diagonal perturbation and Bourgain-Goldstein-style discrete arguments do not transfer directly because the spectrum is unbounded and bad frequencies cannot be fully removed.
examples.engagement_reason: The continuum/PDE case is contrasted by stated features such as unbounded spectrum and lack of the same Lyapunov-exponent framework, without a concrete calculation on it.

passes.reason: The continuum/PDE case is invoked to show the discrete perturbative/localization logic and almost-every-frequency conclusions do not extend.
The continuum/PDE Schrödinger setting is invoked to show the discrete perturbative localization logic does not extend.
The continuum/PDE setting is structurally ordinary but is invoked as an obstruction to the discrete diagonal-perturbation/localization argument.
The continuum/PDE Schrödinger setting is an ordinary unbounded-spectrum case invoked to show the lattice perturbative localization logic does not carry over.
the continuum/PDE case is an ordinary unbounded Schrödinger setting invoked as an obstruction to extending the discrete perturbative localization argument
the continuum/PDE model is a typical unbounded-spectrum setting invoked as an obstruction to the discrete perturbative localization argument
the continuum quasi-periodic Schrödinger/PDE case is an ordinary continuum setting used to show the discrete perturbative localization logic does not extend

The continuum quasi-periodic/PDE case is cited as an obstruction to transferring the discrete diagonal-perturbation/localization argument, while being structurally ordinary for continuum Schrödinger theory.
The continuum/PDE model is raised as an obstruction where the discrete diagonal-perturbation/localization logic and theorem do not carry over.
Used to show the lattice perturbative/localization logic and almost-every-frequency statements do not carry over to the continuum/PDE setting.
The continuum/PDE Schrödinger setting is an ordinary unbounded-spectrum setting invoked to show the discrete perturbative/localization argument does not carry over.
The continuum/PDE analogue is invoked to show the discrete perturbative/localization reasoning and almost-every-frequency conclusions cannot simply carry over.

examples.name: Constant sampling function as a degenerate potential
examples.note: The constant-potential case is mentioned only to make clear that the positive Lyapunov-exponent hypothesis excludes a trivial zero-exponent situation.
examples.engagement_reason: The constant sampling function is only cited as a degenerate excluded case because it would make the Lyapunov exponent zero.

passes.reason: A constant sampling function is a translation-invariant zero-variation potential that defeats the positive-Lyapunov nondegeneracy assumption.
A constant sampling function is the zero-disorder translation-invariant potential giving zero Lyapunov exponent, excluding the desired nondegenerate positive-LE case.
A constant sampling function has no potential variation and forces zero Lyapunov exponent, showing why a nondegeneracy assumption is needed.
A constant sampling function is a fully translation-invariant collapsed potential and cannot satisfy the positive-Lyapunov nondegeneracy hypothesis.
a constant sampling function is a fully collapsed translation-invariant potential with zero Lyapunov exponent, excluding it from the nondegenerate theorem
a constant sampling function removes the potential variation and forces the nondegeneracy/positive-exponent assumption to fail
a constant sampling function is the zero-variation translation-invariant boundary case giving zero Lyapunov exponent and violating the needed nondegeneracy
A constant sampling function is the collapsed, translation-invariant potential excluded because it would make the required positive Lyapunov behavior fail.
A constant sampling function collapses the potential to a translation-invariant degenerate case with zero Lyapunov exponent, excluding the positivity assumption.
Used to explain why the nondegeneracy/positive-Lyapunov assumption excludes constants; a constant potential is translation-invariant, collapsed, and zero-oscillation/minimal.
A constant sampling function collapses the potential to a translation-invariant trivial case with zero Lyapunov behavior, excluding it from the positive-exponent hypothesis.
A constant sampling function is a collapsed maximally translation-invariant potential for which the Lyapunov exponent is zero, showing the nondegeneracy/positivity condition is necessary.

examples.name: Independent random points and Poisson counts on the circle
examples.note: The Poisson limit for random points supplies the heuristic model for why the fractional parts of j^2 omega might behave randomly enough to force positivity in the skew-shift problem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker sets up random uniform points on the circle, counts hits in an interval of length lambda/n, and identifies the Poisson limiting probabilities.

passes.reason: Independent uniform points with Poisson rare-event counts are used as a standard random heuristic rather than as a structurally special or refuting case.
Independent uniform points with Poisson rare-event counts are the standard random benchmark for the skew-shift heuristic.
Iid uniform points/Poisson counts form a rotation-invariant homogeneous random model used as a heuristic comparison, not as a failure case.
Iid uniform circle points with Poisson rare-event counts are used as the standard random benchmark for comparison, not as an adversarial case.
i.i.d. uniform points give a homogeneous rotation-invariant circle process used as a Poisson-limit model, not as a failure case
iid uniform points with Poisson rare-event counts are a standard random comparison model for Poissonian statistics
iid uniform points give the homogeneous rotation-invariant Poisson benchmark rather than an obstruction to a claim
Independent uniform points with Poisson rare-count limits are used as the standard random comparison model for Poissonian behavior, not as a failure case.
IID uniform points with Poisson rare-event counts are used as the standard probabilistic benchmark for Poissonian behavior, not to disprove a claim.
Used only as the standard iid uniform benchmark for Poissonian behavior of spacings/counts on the circle.
Independent uniform points give the rotation-invariant random benchmark for Poisson counts, used as an analogy for quadratic-orbit statistics.
Independent uniform points give the standard rotation-invariant random model whose rare interval counts have a Poisson limit, not an obstruction to a claim.
